text: minimally invasive Ivor Lewis esophagectomy for esophageal cancer after gastric bypass, performed by the Division of Minimally Invasive Surgery at the University of Florida. Our patient is a fifty-five year old female, five years status post laparoscopic gastric bypass surgery, who was referred to our institution after endoscopy revealed a seven centimeter esophageal adenocarcinoma located at thirty centimeters from the incisors. Her PET-CT showed no distant disease, and an endoscopic ultrasound showed a T-III-N-I lesion. The patient received neoadjuvant chemoradiation, and a good response was observed on her follow-up PET-CT with a decrease in maximum SUV.

The surgeon places abdominal ports and completes adhesiolysis to identify the roux limb and gastric remnant. The liver is freed from the roux limb for retraction and exposure of the diaphragmatic hiatus. The gastric pouch and roux limb are dissected from the gastric remnant, avoiding the gastrocolic omentum to prevent injury to the right gastroepiploic artery. The gastric remnant, gastric pouch, and roux limb are visible. Anterior traction is applied to the gastric pouch, dividing attachments up to the left gastric artery. The right cruce is exposed, and the phrenoesophageal ligament is divided. Circumferential mobilization of the esophagus is carried cephalad into the mediastinum. The right cruce and central tendon are incised to widen the hiatus. Remaining adhesions to the left side of the gastric pouch are divided, completing mobilization of the esophagus, gastric pouch, and roux limb. The jejunum is divided distal to the gastrojejunostomy using an endoscopic linear stapler. Mesenteric attachments to the jejunum are divided, elevating the specimen to reveal the left gastric artery. A celiac nodal dissection is performed, skeletonizing the left gastric artery and reflecting nodal tissue towards the stomach. The left gastric artery is transected with a linear stapler. A Penrose drain is placed around the esophagus and secured. The gastric remnant is mobilized to construct the gastric conduit, preserving the gastroepiploic pedicle. A pyloroplasty is performed, and the gastric conduit is shaped by stapling along the lesser curvature with multiple firings of an endoscopic linear stapler, ensuring symmetry. The distal end of the specimen is sutured to the gastric conduit for delivery into the thoracic cavity. A sixteen French jejunostomy tube is placed in the roux limb and secured to the abdominal wall.

The patient is positioned in the left lateral decubitus position, and thoracic ports are placed. The surgeon divides the inferior pulmonary ligament and incises the pleura along the azygos vein, which arches over the esophagus. The Penrose drain is delivered into the chest to aid in mobilizing the thoracic esophagus. The esophagus is mobilized circumferentially up to the azygos vein, along with lymphatic tissues. The azygos vein is dissected and divided using a linear stapler with a curved tip. The proximal esophagus is transected with a stapler, and the gastric conduit is gently delivered into the chest. The specimen, including the esophagus, gastric pouch, and a few centimeters of jejunum, is removed. The Orville device is used to deliver the anvil into the proximal esophagus, and a gastrotomy is made in the gastric conduit for a 25mm EEA stapler. A stapler anastomosis is performed, and the gastrotomy is closed with a stapler, completing the esophagogastric anastomosis.

An esophagram on post-operative day four shows a well-formed gastric conduit with no signs of leakage. The final pathology report indicates no residual tumor and negative surgical margins, with eighteen lymph nodes testing negative.
